infobox.value: (March 7, 1811 – September 14, 1875) was an American writer, scientist, and [naturalist](/wiki/Natural_history "Natural history"), whose work focused primarily on the what is now the U.S. state of [Wisconsin](/wiki/Wisconsin "Wisconsin").[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2) He made maps of the area and published numerous books on the archaeology, biology, and geology of the region, and discovered both the [Panther Intaglio Effigy Mound](/wiki/Panther_Intaglio_Effigy_Mound "Panther Intaglio Effigy Mound") and [Milwaukee Formation](/wiki/Milwaukee_Formation "Milwaukee Formation"). He founded the Wisconsin Natural History Association, and served as the state's Chief Geologist for two years. He also lobbied Congress and the Smithsonian Institution to establish an agency to predict the weather around the [Great Lakes](/wiki/Great_Lakes "Great Lakes"), and these efforts led to what is today the [National Weather Service](/wiki/National_Weather_Service "National Weather Service").[[3]](#cite_note-Miller-3)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

American scientist (1811–1875)

| Increase A. Lapham | |
| --- | --- |
| Lapham examining a [meteorite](/wiki/Meteorite "Meteorite") which had fallen in Wisconsin in 1868 | |
| Born | March 7, 1811  [Palmyra, New York](/wiki/Palmyra_(town),_New_York "Palmyra (town), New York"), US |
| Died | September 14, 1875(1875-09-14) (aged 64)  [Oconomowoc](/wiki/Oconomowoc,_Wisconsin "Oconomowoc, Wisconsin"), Wisconsin, US |
| Known for | Natural history in [Wisconsin](/wiki/Wisconsin "Wisconsin") |
| **Scientific career** | |
| Fields | Ecology, natural history, biology, geography |
| [Author abbrev. (botany)](/wiki/Author_citation_(botany) "Author citation (botany)") | Lapham |
|  | |

**Increase Allen Lapham** (March 7, 1811 – September 14, 1875) was an American writer, scientist, and [naturalist](/wiki/Natural_history "Natural history"), whose work focused primarily on the what is now the U.S. state of [Wisconsin](/wiki/Wisconsin "Wisconsin").[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2) He made maps of the area and published numerous books on the archaeology, biology, and geology of the region, and discovered both the [Panther Intaglio Effigy Mound](/wiki/Panther_Intaglio_Effigy_Mound "Panther Intaglio Effigy Mound") and [Milwaukee Formation](/wiki/Milwaukee_Formation "Milwaukee Formation"). He founded the Wisconsin Natural History Association, and served as the state's Chief Geologist for two years. He also lobbied Congress and the Smithsonian Institution to establish an agency to predict the weather around the [Great Lakes](/wiki/Great_Lakes "Great Lakes"), and these efforts led to what is today the [National Weather Service](/wiki/National_Weather_Service "National Weather Service").[[3]](#cite_note-Miller-3)

## Biography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Increase_A._Lapham&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Biography")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bc/Nuculites_laphami.jpg/250px-Nuculites_laphami.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nuculites_laphami.jpg)

The bivalve *Nuculites laphami* (Cleland, 1911) from the Milwaukee Formation, Middle Devonian, Wisconsin.

Born in [Palmyra, New York](/wiki/Palmyra_(town),_New_York "Palmyra (town), New York"), his family moved to [Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania"), back to New York, to [Ohio](/wiki/Ohio "Ohio") then to [Louisville, Kentucky](/wiki/Louisville,_Kentucky "Louisville, Kentucky") (1827–1830) then back to Ohio while his father, Seneca Lapham, worked on the [canals](/wiki/Canal "Canal") in various locations. Lapham was of entirely [English ancestry](/wiki/English_American "English American"), all of which had been in what is now the United States since the early 1600s. His ancestors were among the first English colonists to establish Rhode Island.[[4]](#cite_note-4) He displayed a talent for scientific observation early on while working on the canals and their [locks](/wiki/Canal_lock "Canal lock"), producing drawings that he could sell at the age of thirteen.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In July 1836, Lapham moved to Kilbourntown (which soon incorporated into the city of [Milwaukee](/wiki/Milwaukee "Milwaukee"), [Wisconsin](/wiki/Wisconsin "Wisconsin")) and worked closely with [Byron Kilbourn](/wiki/Byron_Kilbourn "Byron Kilbourn") in his business and development endeavors.[[5]](#cite_note-summit9-5) The two had worked together previously on the [Miami Canal](/wiki/Miami_and_Erie_Canal "Miami and Erie Canal") and Lapham considered him a loyal friend and mentor. Before the end of the year, Lapham had published a *Catalogue of Plants and Shells, Found in the vicinity of Milwaukee, on the West Side of [Lake Michigan](/wiki/Lake_Michigan "Lake Michigan")*, perhaps the first scientific work published west of the [Great Lakes](/wiki/Great_Lakes "Great Lakes").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In 1848,[[6]](#cite_note-6) Lapham founded the Wisconsin Natural History Association, a predecessor of the Wisconsin Academy of Sciences, Arts, and Letters,[[7]](#cite_note-7) of which he also was a charter member.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*][[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9)

Many of his works and early maps were used for various civil projects such as canal and railroad development. In 1844 Lapham published the first substantial book on the geography of the Wisconsin Territory. His first map of Wisconsin was made in 1846.[[10]](#cite_note-10) He published many more papers and books through his life, particularly on geology, [archaeology](/wiki/Archaeology "Archaeology") and history, and [flora](/wiki/Flora_(plants) "Flora (plants)") and [fauna](/wiki/Fauna_(animals) "Fauna (animals)") of [Wisconsin](/wiki/Wisconsin "Wisconsin"), including publication by the [Smithsonian Institution](/wiki/Smithsonian_Institution "Smithsonian Institution").[[11]](#cite_note-11)

In 1850, he discovered the [Panther Intaglio Effigy Mound](/wiki/Panther_Intaglio_Effigy_Mound "Panther Intaglio Effigy Mound"), which is now listed on the [National Register of Historic Places](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places "National Register of Historic Places").[[12]](#cite_note-12)

Lapham was elected a member of the [American Antiquarian Society](/wiki/American_Antiquarian_Society "American Antiquarian Society") in 1853,[[13]](#cite_note-13) and he was Chief Geologist of the State of Wisconsin from 1873 to 1875.[[14]](#cite_note-GassKluessendorfMikulicBrett2019-14) He was elected as a member to the [American Philosophical Society](/wiki/American_Philosophical_Society "American Philosophical Society") in 1874.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

Lapham was one of the first people to recognize the cement potential of certain rock strata seen along the banks of the Milwaukee River. Those strata, now known as belonging to the [Milwaukee Formation](/wiki/Milwaukee_Formation "Milwaukee Formation"), were later mined for high quality natural hydraulic cement. Milwaukee thus went on to become the country's leading producer of natural hydraulic cement from 1876 to 1910.[[14]](#cite_note-GassKluessendorfMikulicBrett2019-14)

## Honors

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Increase_A._Lapham&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Honors")]

Lapham is considered "Wisconsin's first great scientist"[[16]](#cite_note-16) and the "Father of the [U.S Weather Service](/wiki/National_Weather_Service "National Weather Service"),"[[17]](#cite_note-17)[[18]](#cite_note-18) based upon his lobbying to Congress and the Smithsonian Institution to create such an agency to forecast storms on the Great Lakes and both coasts.[[3]](#cite_note-Miller-3) When the agency was created through the U.S. Secretary of War, Lapham made the first such accurate Great Lakes storm warning from Chicago.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

Since his death, numerous landmarks throughout the southeastern Wisconsin area have been named after him, including [Lapham Peak](/wiki/Lapham_Peak "Lapham Peak"), the highest point in [Waukesha County, Wisconsin](/wiki/Waukesha_County,_Wisconsin "Waukesha County, Wisconsin"),[[20]](#cite_note-20) a major [University of Wisconsin–Milwaukee](/wiki/University_of_Wisconsin%E2%80%93Milwaukee "University of Wisconsin–Milwaukee") building,[[21]](#cite_note-21) and streets.[[22]](#cite_note-22) In [Madison, Wisconsin](/wiki/Madison,_Wisconsin "Madison, Wisconsin"), he currently has an elementary school named after him.[[23]](#cite_note-23)[[24]](#cite_note-24)

A genus of North American plants, *[Laphamia](/wiki/Laphamia "Laphamia")*,[[25]](#cite_note-25) was named for him by [Asa Gray](/wiki/Asa_Gray "Asa Gray").[[26]](#cite_note-26) Several species of [invertebrates](/wiki/Invertebrate "Invertebrate") from the [Paleozoic](/wiki/Paleozoic "Paleozoic") rocks of Wisconsin, such as *Nuculites laphami* (Cleland, 1911)[[14]](#cite_note-GassKluessendorfMikulicBrett2019-14) and *Ekwanoscutellum laphami* (Whitfield, 1877),[[27]](#cite_note-BerglandHayes2014-27) were also named in honor of him. Certain markings found on iron meteors were designated by [J. Lawrence Smith](/wiki/J._Lawrence_Smith_(chemist) "J. Lawrence Smith (chemist)")[[28]](#cite_note-28) as *Laphamite markings*.[[29]](#cite_note-29) A formerly existing [glacial lake](/wiki/Glacial_lake "Glacial lake") was provisionally named *Lake Lapham*.[[30]](#cite_note-30) The Wisconsin Archeological Society awards the Lapham Research Medal,[[31]](#cite_note-31) first doing so in 1926.[[32]](#cite_note-32) The U.S. Navy named a ship [SS Increase A. Lapham](/wiki/SS_Increase_A._Lapham "SS Increase A. Lapham") during World War II.[[33]](#cite_note-33) The University of Wisconsin has an Increase A. Lapham Professorship.[[34]](#cite_note-34) Lapham was inducted in 1992 into the Wisconsin Conservation Hall of Fame[[35]](#cite_note-35) and in 2003 into the [Wisconsin Forestry Hall of Fame](/wiki/Wisconsin_Society_of_American_Foresters "Wisconsin Society of American Foresters").[[36]](#cite_note-36)

The centennial of Lapham's birth was celebrated in 1911.[[37]](#cite_note-37) In 2011, celebration of the bicentennial was planned, including an Increase A. Lapham Day at [Aztalan State Park](/wiki/Aztalan_State_Park "Aztalan State Park").[[38]](#cite_note-38)

Lapham Junction ([44°17′51″N 90°28′41″W﻿ / ﻿44.29750°N 90.47806°W﻿ / 44.29750; -90.47806﻿ (Lapham Junction)](https://geohack.toolforge.org/geohack.php?pagename=Increase_A._Lapham&params=44_17_51_N_90_28_41_W_region:US-WI&title=Lapham+Junction)) in [Knapp](/wiki/Knapp,_Jackson_County,_Wisconsin "Knapp, Jackson County, Wisconsin"), [Jackson County](/wiki/Jackson_County,_Wisconsin "Jackson County, Wisconsin"), [Wisconsin](/wiki/Wisconsin "Wisconsin"), is now defunct railroad junction on the [Goodyear branch](/wiki/Charles_W._Goodyear "Charles W. Goodyear") of the [Milwaukee Road](/wiki/Chicago,_Milwaukee,_St._Paul_and_Pacific_Railroad "Chicago, Milwaukee, St. Paul and Pacific Railroad") which branch ran to Zeda, where lumbering operations took place,[[39]](#cite_note-39)[[40]](#cite_note-40) and was named for Lapham.[[41]](#cite_note-41)

## Selected works

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Increase_A._Lapham&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Selected works")]

Some works of Increase A. Lapham:

| Title | Date |  |
| --- | --- | --- |
| *Notice of the Louisville and Shipping sport Canal and of the Geology of the vicinity* | 1828 | [[42]](#cite_note-42) |
| *A Catalogue of Plants & Shells, Found in the Vicinity of Milwaukee, on the West Side of Lake Michigan* | 1836 | [[43]](#cite_note-43) |
| *A Geographical and Topographical Description of Wisconsin* | 1844 | [[44]](#cite_note-44) |
| *Wisconsin: its geography and topography, history, geology, and mineralogy* | 1846 | [[45]](#cite_note-45) |
| *Fauna and Flora of Wisconsin* | 1852 | [[46]](#cite_note-46) |
| *The Antiquities of Wisconsin* | 1855 | [[47]](#cite_note-47) |
| *On the Man-shaped Mounds of Wisconsin* | 1859 | [[48]](#cite_note-48) |
| *Opening an Ancient Mound Near Madison, Wisconsin* | 1860 | [[49]](#cite_note-49) |
| *Geological Map of Wisconsin* | 1855 | [[50]](#cite_note-50) |
| *Report on the Disastrous Effects of the Destruction of Forest Trees* | 1867 | [[51]](#cite_note-51) |
| *New Geological Map of Wisconsin* | 1869 | [[52]](#cite_note-52) |
| *Oconomowoc and other Small Lakes of Wisconsin* | 1876 | [[53]](#cite_note-53) |

The standard [author abbreviation](/wiki/List_of_botanists_by_author_abbreviation_(A) "List of botanists by author abbreviation (A)") Lapham is used to indicate this person as the author when [citing](/wiki/Author_citation_(botany) "Author citation (botany)") a [botanical name](/wiki/Botanical_name "Botanical name").[[54]](#cite_note-54)
